title: Deep-Copy IntArray
prompt_md:
## Goal

The `IntArray` class owns a heap-allocated `int` array (`new[]` in the constructor, `delete[]` in the destructor). The compiler-generated copy operations would shallow-copy the pointer, causing aliasing and a double-delete. Give it a copy constructor and a copy assignment operator that perform a **deep copy**, so every copy owns independent memory.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 100), the array size.
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers (each between -1000 and 1000).
- Line 3: two integers `idx` and `val` (0 <= idx < n).

## Output

`main` builds `original`, makes `copy` via the copy constructor and `assigned` via the assignment operator, then mutates `original[idx] = val`. If your copies are deep, they are unaffected.

- Line 1: all elements of `copy`, each followed by a space.
- Line 2: all elements of `assigned`, each followed by a space.

The assignment operator must guard against self-assignment and release its existing buffer before allocating a new one. Do not modify `main` or the existing members.

test_cases.label: Mutate first element of larger array

title: Rule of Three: Named String
prompt_md:
## Goal

The `NamedValue` class owns a C-string on the heap (`new char[]`, freed in the destructor) plus an integer. With a custom destructor present, the Rule of Three says it also needs a custom copy constructor and copy assignment operator. Implement both so copies are independent.

## Input

- Line 1: a single word (up to 49 characters) and an integer, space-separated.

## Output

`main` builds an object, copies it, then changes the original's value. With correct deep copies:

- Line 1: `<label> <value>` of the copy (unchanged).
- Line 2: `<label> <value>` of the original (modified).

For the string member, allocate `std::strlen(src) + 1` chars and copy with `std::strcpy`. The assignment operator must guard self-assignment and free its old buffer first. Include `<cstring>`. Do not modify `main` or the existing members.
